questions.question: Explain the difference between early binding and late binding with an example.
keyPoints: Early binding occurs at compile time while late binding occurs at runtime
Early binding used for function overloading, late binding for virtual functions
Example: Function overloading uses early binding, virtual function calls use late binding
Early binding is faster, late binding provides flexibility

questions.question: What is the role of VTABLE and VPTR in achieving runtime polymorphism?
keyPoints: VTABLE is a lookup table created for classes with virtual functions
VTABLE contains addresses of virtual function implementations
VPTR is a hidden pointer in each object pointing to class VTABLE
At runtime, VPTR is used to look up function address in VTABLE for dynamic dispatch

questions.question: What is a pure virtual function? How does it differ from a virtual function?
keyPoints: Pure virtual function declared with '= 0' has no implementation
Pure virtual function makes class abstract
Virtual function has implementation in base class and can be overridden
Abstract class cannot be instantiated but can have constructors

questions.question: Why are virtual destructors important in C++? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Virtual destructors ensure proper cleanup when derived object deleted via base pointer
Without virtual destructor, only base destructor is called causing memory leak
Virtual destructor allows calling derived destructor first then base destructor
Should be declared virtual in base classes with virtual functions

questions.question: What is object slicing? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Object slicing occurs when derived object assigned to base object by value
Slicing removes derived portion, loses polymorphism
Prevented by using pointers or references to base class
Cannot slice when using virtual functions through pointers/references

questions.question: Explain runtime polymorphism with an example program showing virtual function usage.
keyPoints: Runtime polymorphism achieved through virtual functions
Function call resolved at runtime based on actual object type
Base class pointer can hold derived class object
Same function call behaves differently based on actual object type

questions.question: What is RTTI? How is it used in C++?
keyPoints: RTTI stands for Runtime Type Information
Provides type information about objects at runtime
Used with dynamic_cast for safe downcasting
Used with typeid to get type name of objects

questions.question: Explain the complete mechanism of late binding in C++ with VTABLE and VPTR. Include a diagram showing how a virtual function call is resolved at runtime.
keyPoints: When a class has virtual functions, compiler creates VTABLE for that class
VTABLE contains function pointers to virtual function implementations
Each object of class contains hidden VPTR pointing to VTABLE
When virtual function called, runtime uses VPTR to access VTABLE and resolve function address
Derived class VTABLE contains pointers to overridden functions
This mechanism enables runtime polymorphism
Performance overhead due to double indirection through VTABLE

questions.question: Write a C++ program demonstrating runtime polymorphism using virtual functions. Also explain how early binding differs from late binding in this context.
keyPoints: Create base class with virtual function
Create derived class overriding the virtual function
Use base class pointer to point to derived object
Call virtual function through base pointer - demonstrates late binding
Show that function call resolved at runtime based on actual object type
Compare with non-virtual function - uses early binding
Explain compile-time vs runtime resolution

questions.question: What is an abstract class? How is it different from concrete class? Explain with example showing pure virtual functions and their role in design.
keyPoints: Abstract class contains at least one pure virtual function
Pure virtual function declared with '= 0' has no implementation
Abstract class cannot be instantiated - only used as base class
Derived classes must override all pure virtual functions to become concrete
Abstract classes define interfaces for derived classes
Example: Shape abstract class with draw() pure virtual, Circle and Rectangle override it
Promotes encapsulation and provides common interface

questions.question: Explain the concept of virtual destructor with an example. Why is it necessary to have virtual destructors in class hierarchies with polymorphism?
keyPoints: Virtual destructor declared using virtual keyword before destructor
When derived object deleted through base pointer, virtual mechanism ensures proper order
Derived destructor called first, then base destructor called
Without virtual: only base destructor called causing resource leak in derived class
Should make base class destructor virtual if class has any virtual functions
Demonstrate with code example showing proper cleanup
Memory leak and undefined behavior without virtual destructor
